claim: Bases is a no-code visual database core feature shipped in Obsidian 1.9.10 that already replaces Dataview for the majority of users, with faster query speed and inline-editable cells that write back to YAML frontmatter; current limitation is table-only views.
quote: Unlike Dataview and Datacore, Bases don't require any coding—you can create and edit Bases entirely with a visual editor... Bases currently only support table views and they can only query data that is stored in properties.

claim: Power users report replacing every Dataview query with Bases tables in 2026.
quote: Users have replaced every single Dataview query with Obsidian Bases, with all the tables they used to roll up notes based on specific queries now replaced.

claim: Templater remains actively maintained at v2.20.0 (April 29 2026) with ~4.9k GitHub stars and is the JavaScript-capable templating engine for Obsidian.
quote: GitHub Star Count: 4,900 stars... latest being version 2.20.0 (April 29, 2026)... 'a template plugin for obsidian' that enables users to 'insert variables and functions results into your notes'

claim: QuickAdd (~2.2k stars, v2.12.0 March 2026) bundles templates, captures, macros, and multi choices for single-keystroke note creation.
quote: GitHub Stars: 2.2k stars, Current Version: 2.12.0 (released March 5, 2026)... four main tools: templates, captures, macros, and multi choices

claim: Excalidraw plugin sits at ~6.8k GitHub stars (v2.22.1, April 2026) with monthly releases, supporting embedded sketches, LaTeX, OCR and a script-engine API.
quote: Stars: 6.8k... Latest Version: 2.22.1 (Released April 26, 2026)... Script engine for automation (ExcalidrawAutomate)

claim: Obsidian Linter (~1.9k stars, v1.31.2 March 2026) auto-formats notes via 80+ rules covering YAML, headings, footnotes and spacing.
quote: GitHub Stars: 1.9k... 80+ customizable rules organized into categories like YAML formatting, heading styles, footnote management... latest version 1.31.2 from March 2026

claim: DB Folder was archived by its owner on July 28 2025 with last release 3.5.1 (Jan 2024); users should treat it as legacy and migrate to Bases or Datacore.
quote: The repository was archived by the owner on July 28, 2025, and is now read-only... The last release (3.5.1) was published on January 19, 2024

claim: Marcusolsson's Obsidian Projects plugin was archived July 18 2025 after the author left Obsidian; a community fork (obsmd-projects) has resurrected it.
quote: The repository was archived by the owner on July 18, 2025, making it read-only. As of May 2025, Marcus Olsson decided to discontinue the plugin... someone has since reanimated the Projects plugin

claim: Smart Connections (~4.9k stars) provides local-by-default semantic note linking and chat with no API key required, with optional access to 100+ models.
quote: Smart Connections has 4.9k stars on GitHub... Operates locally by default with a built-in embedding model—no API keys required... Supports 100+ AI models via APIs

claim: logancyang/obsidian-copilot (~6.8k stars) offers ChatGPT-style vault chat plus an agentic Plus tier with web/YouTube tools, PDF/EPUB ingestion and side-by-side diff editing.
quote: GitHub Stars: 6.8k stars... Agent mode that automatically calls relevant tools (vault search, web search, YouTube)... PDF, EPUB, and image processing

claim: Obsidian Web Clipper (~4.1k stars, v1.6.2 April 2026) is the official browser extension capturing pages to Markdown with template logic, highlighting, and 2026 additions for YouTube transcripts and improved Reader Mode.
quote: GitHub Stars: 4.1k... 'Template logic (if/for)' and variable support... latest version at 1.6.2 (April 2026)

claim: The 2026 Web Clipper update added YouTube transcript capture and an improved Reader Mode, drawing strong community traction.
quote: The 2026 update brings YouTube transcript capture and enhanced Reader Mode, which transform video learning and web research for knowledge workers.

claim: Foam (VS Code, ~17.1k stars, v0.38.0 April 2026) gives developers a Git-native PKM with wiki-links, graph view, daily notes and orphan detection — strongest fit when notes already live next to source.
quote: GitHub Stars: 17.1k... Latest Release: v0.38.0 (April 16, 2026)... Graph visualization to display note connections, Wiki-style link autocompletion... Orphan and placeholder detection

claim: For developers wanting CLI integration, large-vault performance and Git-friendly Markdown, Obsidian wins over Logseq; Logseq's outliner suits rapid block capture but its flat structure can feel chaotic for reference material.
quote: Developers who live in the terminal and want automation hooks, CLI integration, large vault performance, and a portable Markdown setup that plays well with Git should go with Obsidian.

claim: SilverBullet (~5.2k stars, v2.6.1 April 2026) is a self-hosted PWA Markdown PKM with Lua scripting, an Objects/Queries database layer and offline-first sync — suits homelab/self-host users.
quote: SilverBullet has 5.2k stars on GitHub... 'a Programmable, Private, Browser-based, Open Source, Self Hosted, Personal Knowledge Management Platform'... latest release is version 2.6.1 (April 11, 2026)

claim: Tana's supertag system imposes typed schemas (fields, dates, references) on outline nodes, turning notes into queryable objects (Project/Person/Task) — strongest fit when structure-first metadata matters more than file portability.
quote: Tana's supertag system... allowing users to define structured fields — text, dates, dropdowns, references to other nodes — and apply them to any outline node. Supertags turn your notes into objects like a Project, Person, Task, Book, Recipe

claim: Reflect is a paid-only ($10/mo) networked-notes app with calendar-bound meeting notes and selectable GPT-4o/Claude Sonnet backends — narrower than Tana for technical PKM but lighter to onboard.
quote: Reflect connects notes directly to your calendar with meeting notes automatically associated with calendar events, and offers GPT-4o and Claude Sonnet as user-selectable AI backends, positioned as a paid-only service at $10/month.

claim: Obsidian's 2026 release of an official CLI extends its developer appeal for scripting and external-tool integration, widening the gap with Logseq for technical workflows.
quote: The 2026 introduction of an Obsidian CLI further extends its appeal to developers who want to automate workflows or integrate their vault into scripts and external tools.
